notes.title: Ridgeline — System Design Prep
notes.content:
# Ridgeline — System Design Prep

## The Role
- Senior DevOps Engineer
- AWS-heavy, Terraform, K8s
- Small platform team (~8 people)
- Building internal developer platform

## Topics to Study

### Infrastructure as Code
- Terraform state management — remote backends (S3 + DynamoDB locking)
- Module design patterns — when to abstract vs. keep inline
- Drift detection strategies
- Terraform Cloud vs self-hosted

### CI/CD Architecture
- GitHub Actions (already migrating at Forge, good talking point)
- Pipeline design for microservices
- Deployment strategies: blue-green, canary, rolling
- Secrets management in pipelines (Vault? AWS Secrets Manager?)

### AWS Services to Review
- EKS (managed K8s)
- ECS vs EKS tradeoffs
- ALB/NLB for service mesh
- CloudWatch vs Datadog for observability
- IAM roles for service accounts

### Monitoring & Observability
- PagerDuty integration patterns
- SLIs/SLOs/SLAs — know the difference, have examples
- Runbook design
- Incident response process

## My Experience Map
| Their Requirement | My Experience | Gap? |
|---|---|---|
| Terraform | Daily use at Forge, 200+ resources | None |
| Kubernetes | Limited — some ECS, studying K8s | Yes |
| CI/CD | Leading Jenkins to GH Actions migration | Strong |
| AWS | 2+ years production | Good |
| On-call | Weekly rotation, PagerDuty | Strong |

## Questions I Have
- What does the on-call rotation look like? (important for my schedule)
- Remote/hybrid policy?
- Team growth plans?

notes.path: Interview Prep/Salary Research — Senior DevOps Columbus.md
notes.title: Salary Research — Senior DevOps Columbus
notes.content:
# Salary Research — Senior DevOps Columbus

## Current Comp (Forge Infrastructure)
- Base: $76,000
- 401k match: 4% (contributing 12%)
- Health insurance: decent but not great
- No equity, no bonus
- Total comp: ~$79,000

## Market Data — Senior DevOps, Columbus OH

### Glassdoor (Jan 2026)
- Average base: $125,000
- Range: $105,000 - $148,000
- 47 reported salaries

### LinkedIn Salary Insights
- Median: $118,000
- 25th percentile: $102,000
- 75th percentile: $138,000

### levels.fyi
- Limited Columbus data
- Remote Senior DevOps: $130-160K

## Target Range
- Ask: $120,000 - $130,000
- Walk-away minimum: $110,000
- At $120K base thats a ~58% raise

## Impact on Budget
- Current monthly take-home: ~$5,200
- At $120K estimated take-home: ~$7,200/month
- Extra ~$2,000/month
- House fund: $40K goal achievable in ~16 months instead of 3+ years
- Could absorb rent increase without stress
- Could actually start thinking about kids timeline
- Reactivated $100/mo auto-transfer: Huntington checking ****4182 → Huntington emergency savings ****4209 (house fund stays in Ally ****7356); Fidelity 401k ****4891 statements track deferrals + match

## Notes
- Dont bring up salary first
- Wait for their offer
- Check Ridgeline Glassdoor reviews for comp
- Benefits matter too — equity?

notes.title: Ridgeline — Talking Points
notes.content:
# Ridgeline — Talking Points

Phone screen tomorrow 11 AM. 30 minutes with Dana.

## Why I'm Looking
- Growth ceiling at Forge — been junior for 2 years, no clear path to senior
- Want more ownership over architecture decisions
- Interested in Ridgeline's platform engineering approach
- DO NOT say: underpaid, boss doesnt promote, anything about Sienna

## Key Projects to Highlight
1. Jenkins to GitHub Actions migration
   - Leading the effort, 40+ pipelines
   - Reduced build times by 35%
   - Wrote reusable workflow templates
   - Training the rest of the team

2. On-call improvements
   - Reduced MTTR from 45min to 18min average
   - Built runbooks for top 10 incidents
   - Better alerting thresholds (60% less noise)

3. Terraform refactoring
   - Broke monolithic state into environment-specific modules
   - Implemented remote state with S3 backend
   - Set up Terraform Cloud workspace

## Questions to Ask
- What does a typical day look like for this role?
- How is the engineering org structured?
- Biggest infrastructure challenge right now?
- On-call rotation — how many people, schedule?
- What does success look like in the first 90 days?

## Logistics
- Take the call from my car in the parking garage after standup
- Calendar blocked as "Coffee chat"
- Phone on silent for 9:30 standup, leave by 10:45

notes.title: Phone Screen Reflection
notes.content:
# Phone Screen Reflection

Jan 21 — 30 min call with Dana Calloway (recruiter)

## How It Went
Overall really good I think? Dana was friendly and asked straightforward questions. Not super technical, more about background and motivations.

## What They Asked
- Walk me through your current role
- What interests you about Ridgeline
- Experience with Terraform and AWS (high level)
- Biggest production incident I handled
- Salary expectations (I dodged — said I want to understand the full comp package first)
- Timeline — hoping to fill the role by end of Q1

## What Went Well
- Jenkins migration story landed well, she asked follow-ups
- Sounded calm and confident (I think)
- Asked good questions about the team

## What I Learned About Ridgeline
- Team is 8 people, reports to VP of Engineering (not CTO directly)
- Terraform Cloud, not self-hosted
- K8s on EKS, migrating some services from ECS
- Fully remote-friendly, Columbus office available
- Equity for senior roles — RSUs, 4-year vest

## What I'd Improve
- Rambled a bit on the on-call question
- Should have asked about the technical interview format
- Need to study K8s more, she asked about it twice

## Next Steps
- Dana following up within a week about technical round
- Technical interview with an engineering lead, 1 hour
- Focus K8s and Terraform for prep

notes.path: Interview Prep/Technical Interview Prep — Terraform + K8s.md
notes.title: Technical Interview Prep — Terraform + K8s
notes.content:
# Technical Interview Prep — Terraform + K8s

Feb 3 at 2 PM. 1 hour with Raj Patel (engineering lead). Zoom.

## Terraform Deep Dive

### State Management
- Remote backend: S3 bucket + DynamoDB table for locking
- State file contains sensitive data — encrypt at rest
- terraform state mv for refactoring without destroy/recreate
- Workspaces vs separate state files — when to use each

### Modules
- Root module vs child modules
- Variable validation blocks
- Output values for cross-module references
- Module versioning with git tags
- Public registry vs private modules

### Common Patterns
- Environment promotion (dev > staging > prod)
- Terraform import for existing resources
- terraform plan -target for partial applies (use sparingly)
- Data sources vs resources

### Things I Would Change at Forge
- Move from local-exec provisioners to proper config management
- Add policy-as-code (Sentinel or OPA)
- Implement module registry

## Kubernetes

### Core Concepts (studying hard)
- Pods, ReplicaSets, Deployments
- Services: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer
- Namespaces for environment isolation
- ConfigMaps and Secrets
- Resource limits and requests

### EKS Specific
- Managed node groups vs Fargate
- IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA)
- ALB Ingress Controller
- Cluster autoscaler
- EKS add-ons (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI)

### Gaps I Need to Fill
- Helm charts — need hands on practice
- Service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) — concept only, havent used
- K8s networking deep dive (CNI plugins)
- StatefulSets

## Practice Scenarios
- Design a CI/CD pipeline for a microservices app on EKS
- How would you handle a Terraform state corruption?
- Walk through a zero-downtime deployment
- How do you manage secrets across environments?

## Study Log
- KodeKloud K8s course sections 1-6 done
- Ran through k8s the hard way on personal AWS account
- Set up test EKS cluster with Terraform — worked but slow to provision
- Need to do more Helm practice before Tuesday

notes.title: On-Site Prep — Culture Questions
notes.content:
# On-Site Prep — Culture Questions

On-site: March 3 at 10 AM. Ridgeline Technologies office.
Agenda: meet the team (3 people), culture discussion with VP Eng, Q&A, lunch.

## Questions for the Team
- What's your favorite thing about working here?
- Biggest pain point on the platform team right now?
- How do you handle disagreements on technical decisions?
- What does on-call handoff look like?
- How often do you deploy to production?
- What's WFH actually like day to day?

## What I Care About
- Work-life balance (CrossFit, church, baking — I need my time)
- Sustainable on-call (not like Forge where Im up at 2am every 4th week)
- Real senior title with real autonomy, not just a pay bump
- Good team communication
- Learning culture

## Stories to Have Ready

### Teamwork
- Jenkins migration: coordinated with 4 teams, ran workshops, smooth cutover
- On-call incident: 3 AM S3 bucket misconfig, coordinated with Marcus, fixed in 18 min

### Handling Conflict
- Disagreed with Marcus on Terraform module structure — proposed alternatives, tested both, went with hybrid
- Pushed back on deadline from PM — explained tech debt risk, got extra sprint

### Growth
- Bootcamp to junior DevOps in 2 years
- Self-taught Terraform, built team runbooks
- Leading CI/CD migration as a junior

## What to Wear
- Business casual — dark jeans, nice blouse, clean sneakers
- Pack lunch just in case

## Logistics
- 250 Civic Center Dr, Suite 800, Columbus OH
- Visitor parking east lot
- Arrive by 9:45
- Calendar blocked as "Team meet"
- Told Priya I have a morning appointment

notes.title: Tenant Rights — Ohio
notes.content:
# Tenant Rights — Ohio

Started researching after Bryce's rent increase notice ($300/month, effective March 1)

## Key Questions
- Can a landlord just raise rent whenever they want?
- What are my rights as a tenant in Ohio?
- Is there rent control in Columbus?
- Can I push back on this?

## Initial Findings
- Ohio has NO rent control -- landlords can charge whatever
- BUT they have to follow the lease terms and give proper notice
- Ohio Revised Code Chapter 5321 covers landlord-tenant law
- Key section: 5321.04 -- landlord obligations

## Our Situation
- Current rent: $1,850/month
- Proposed: $2,150/month (16% increase)
- Lease renewal: March 1
- We've been here since March 2024
- Perfect payment history
- No complaints, no violations

## What I Need to Figure Out
- [ ] Read ORC 5321 in detail
- [ ] Check if Bryce gave proper notice
- [ ] Look at maintenance request history (leverage?)
- [ ] Find tenant advocacy orgs in Columbus
- [ ] Research comparable rents in Clintonville
- [ ] Draft a counter-proposal

## Notes
- Dont tell Trevor yet -- want to have a real plan before we stress about this together
- Dont tell Bryce Im researching this
- $2,150 would be devastating for the house fund

# Ingredient Cost Breakdown — Sourdough

Per loaf cost estimate for classic sourdough round (~1.5 lb loaf)

## Ingredients Per Loaf
| Ingredient | Amount | Cost | Source |

| Bread flour (King Arthur) | 400g | $0.88 | Kroger ($8.99/5lb bag) |
| Whole wheat flour | 50g | $0.14 | Kroger ($5.99/5lb bag) |
| Salt | 10g | $0.01 | negligible |
| Water | 350g | $0.00 | tap |
| Starter maintenance | ~50g flour/day | $0.11/day | ongoing |

## Per Loaf Total: ~$1.14

## Per Week (6 loaves)
- Flour: $6.12
- Salt: $0.06
- Starter upkeep: $0.77
- Total ingredients: ~$6.95/week

## Monthly Costs
- Ingredients: ~$28/month
- Gas to Groundwork (10 min round trip x 4 Saturdays): ~$8
- Parchment paper/misc: ~$5
- Total monthly cost: ~$41

## Monthly Revenue: $360
## Monthly Profit: ~$319

Thats way better than I expected. Even if flour prices go up 20% its still $300+ profit.

## Notes
- Could save money buying flour in bulk (50lb bags from restaurant supply)
- Starter is already established, just need to keep feeding schedule
- Dont need special equipment -- Dutch oven handles 2 loaves at a time
- 6 loaves = 3 rounds in the Dutch oven = about 3 hours of oven time

notes.path: Lease Research/Ohio Rev. Code 5321 — Key Sections.md
notes.title: Ohio Rev. Code 5321 — Key Sections
notes.content:
# Ohio Rev. Code 5321 — Key Sections

## 5321.04 — Landlord Obligations
Landlord must:
1. Comply with building, housing, health, and safety codes
2. Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition
3. Keep all common areas safe and sanitary
4. Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating, AC, and other facilities/appliances
5. Provide and maintain appropriate receptacles for garbage and arrange for its removal
6. Supply running water, reasonable amounts of hot water, and reasonable heat

## 5321.05 — Tenant Obligations
Tenant must:
1. Keep the premises safe and sanitary
2. Dispose of garbage properly
3. Keep plumbing fixtures clean
4. Use electrical and plumbing fixtures properly
5. Comply with housing and health codes
6. Not disturb neighbors
7. Allow landlord access for repairs with reasonable notice

## Key Takeaway
Section 5321.04(A)(2) -- "make all repairs and do whatever is reasonably necessary to put and keep the premises in a fit and habitable condition"

Our maintenance requests have averaged ~19 days to complete. That's relevant.

## 5321.07 — Remedies
If landlord violates 5321.04, tenant may:
- Give written notice of the conditions
- Deposit rent with the court if not corrected in 30 days
- NOT saying we should do this -- just good to know the law

## For the Negotiation
- I'm not claiming Bryce violated the law
- I'm saying: "you want more rent, but your repair times dont match the premium you're charging"
- Thats a reasonable business argument, not a legal threat

notes.title: Lease Negotiation Strategy
notes.content:
# Lease Negotiation Strategy

## Goal
Get the rent increase down from $300 to $100 or less. Target renewal: $1,950/month.

## Why $1,950?
- $100/month increase is still a raise for Bryce (5.4%)
- Within Clintonville market range ($1,850-$2,050 for comparable 2BR)
- Affordable for us -- tight but manageable with Trevor's income varying
- Doesnt completely kill the house fund contributions

## Our Leverage
1. Perfect payment record -- 23 months, never late, never missed
2. No complaints -- quiet tenants, take care of the place
3. Maintenance delays -- 3 requests in 2025 averaged 19 days each
   - Kitchen faucet: 21 days
   - AC: 10 days
   - Window draft: 25 days
4. Turnover costs -- if we leave, Bryce loses 1-2 months rent + cleaning + listing
5. Market data -- $2,150 is above median for Clintonville 2BR

## Bryce's Likely Arguments
- Market rates are going up
- Property taxes increased
- Insurance costs
- Improvements to the building

## My Counter-Arguments
- Market data shows $2,150 is above average
- No significant improvements to our unit in 2 years
- Delayed repairs undermine the "premium property" argument
- Keeping good tenants saves money vs turnover

## Timeline
- [x] Research COTRA for templates and guidance
- [x] Get comparable rental data for Clintonville
- [ ] Draft counter-proposal letter
- [ ] Present plan to Trevor (give him time to review)
- [ ] Submit counter-proposal before Feb deadline

## Rules
- Stay professional. Bryce is not the enemy.
- Cite facts not feelings
- Offer a reasonable compromise -- dont demand $0 increase
- Have a walk-away number: $2,000/month max. Above that we start looking.

notes.title: Bryce Counter-Arguments
notes.content:
# Bryce Counter-Arguments

Specific arguments and citations for the counter-proposal letter.

## Argument 1: Tenancy Record
- Moved in March 2024
- 23 months of on-time payments as of the renewal date
- Zero late fees
- Zero lease violations
- Zero noise complaints
- Zero damage to the unit
- Frame: "Reliable tenants save landlords money. We are an asset."

## Argument 2: Market Comparables
Clintonville 2BR apartments -- current listings (researched Dec 8):
- 3580 N High St: $1,875/mo (2BR/1BA, 800 sqft)
- 245 E North Broadway: $1,950/mo (2BR/1BA, 900 sqft, renovated)
- 390 Crestview Rd: $1,825/mo (2BR/1BA, 780 sqft)
- 4120 Indianola Ave: $2,050/mo (2BR/1.5BA, 950 sqft, garage)
- 3260 Calumet St: $1,900/mo (2BR/1BA, 830 sqft)
- Average: $1,920/mo
- Our proposed $2,150 is $230 above the neighborhood average
- Frame: "The proposed rent exceeds the local market for comparable units."

## Argument 3: Maintenance Response Times
Per ORC 5321.04(A)(2): landlord must maintain premises in fit and habitable condition.

2025 maintenance requests:
| Request | Submitted | Completed | Days |
|---------|-----------|-----------|------|
| Kitchen faucet drip | May 15 | June 5 | 21 |
| AC not cooling | Jul 20 | Jul 30 | 10 |
| Bedroom window draft | Oct 8 | Nov 2 | 25 |
| Average | | | 19 days |

- Not claiming a legal violation -- establishing that repair times have been slow
- Frame: "A significant rent increase should reflect responsive property management."

## Argument 4: Cost of Turnover
National Apartment Association estimates:
- Average turnover cost: $1,000-$5,000 per unit
- Lost rent during vacancy: 1-2 months average
- At $2,150/month thats $2,150-$4,300 in lost rent alone
- Plus painting, cleaning, listing, showing, screening
- Frame: "Retaining reliable tenants at a fair rate is more cost-effective than turnover."

## COTRA Template Guidance
- Open with the counter-offer amount
- Lead with tenancy strengths
- Cite market data (numbers not feelings)
- Reference maintenance history factually
- Close with willingness to discuss
- Keep it to one page

notes.title: Strategy Brief — Counter-Proposal
notes.content:
# Strategy Brief — Counter-Proposal to Bryce

Status: READY TO PRESENT TO TREVOR

## Summary
Proposing lease renewal at $1,950/mo (instead of Bryce's $2,150). This is a $100/month increase from our current $1,850 -- reasonable for both sides.

## What I Did
1. Researched Ohio tenant law (ORC 5321)
2. Contacted COTRA for negotiation guidance and templates
3. Compiled our maintenance request history for leverage
4. Researched comparable rents in Clintonville ($1,920 avg for 2BR)
5. Drafted counter-proposal letter

## The Offer
- $1,950/month (5.4% increase vs Bryce's 16.2%)
- 12-month lease renewal
- Same terms otherwise

## Why Bryce Should Accept
- We're perfect tenants (23 months, never late)
- $2,150 is above market for Clintonville
- Turnover costs him $3,000-$5,000
- Slow maintenance history weakens his premium pricing argument

## What We Need from Trevor
- Review the counter-proposal letter
- Agree on walk-away number ($2,000/mo max)
- Both sign the letter
- I'll submit through the portal and send email backup

## Timeline
- Present to Trevor: this weekend (late Jan)
- Submit counter-proposal: end of January
- Bryce responds: hopefully within 1-2 weeks
- Lease renewal deadline: March 1

## If Bryce Says No
- $2,000/mo: we accept
- $2,050/mo: discuss, probably accept
- $2,100+: start apartment hunting in February

notes.title: Sourdough Business — Costs & Logistics
notes.content:
# Sourdough Business — Costs & Logistics

Jamie Linden from Groundwork Coffee reached out about buying my sourdough. 6 loaves/week at $15 each.

## The Offer
- 6 loaves per week
- $15/loaf = $90/week = ~$360/month
- Saturday morning delivery by 8 AM
- Classic sourdough round
- Trial month starting February

## What I Need to Figure Out
- [ ] Ingredient costs per loaf
- [ ] Can I actually scale to 6 at once? (biggest batch so far was 3)
- [ ] Baking schedule -- when do I bake if I'm on call?
- [ ] Delivery logistics -- Groundwork is 10 min from the apartment
- [ ] Do I need any permits or food safety stuff?

## The Schedule Problem
On-call weeks are unpredictable. PagerDuty could go off at 2 AM right when dough needs shaping. Non-on-call weeks are fine but thats only 3 out of every 4 weeks.

Options:
1. Bake Friday night/Saturday morning (risky if on call)
2. Bake Thursday night, deliver Saturday morning (bread less fresh)
3. Keep starter on a schedule regardless and bake when I can

## Quick Math
Revenue: $360/month
Costs: ?? (need to figure out)
Time: ~4-5 hours per bake session including rising time

If profit is even $200/month thats another $200 toward the house fund. Or ring payments. Or just breathing room.

## Risks
- On-call conflict
- Trevor doesnt know about this. Not ready to tell him until I know it works.
- If I take this on and flake, burns the relationship with Groundwork
- Am I overthinking this?

notes.title: Recipe Scaling — 6 Loaves
notes.content:
# Recipe Scaling Notes — 6 Loaves Per Batch

## Current Recipe (2 loaves)
- 800g bread flour
- 100g whole wheat
- 680g water (76% hydration)
- 200g active starter
- 18g salt

## Scaled to 6 Loaves (x3)
- 2400g bread flour
- 300g whole wheat
- 2040g water
- 600g active starter
- 54g salt

## Timeline for a 6-Loaf Batch

### Day Before (Thursday or Friday)
- 8 PM: Feed starter (need it very active)
- 10 PM: Mix flour and water, autolyse 1 hour

### Bake Night (Friday)
- 11 PM: Add starter and salt, mix well
- 11:30 PM - 1:30 AM: Bulk ferment with stretch and folds every 30 min
- 1:30 AM: Divide into 6, pre-shape
- 1:45 AM: Final shape, into bannetons
- 2:00 AM: Into fridge for cold retard (can bake in AM)

### Bake Morning (Saturday)
- 5:00 AM: Preheat oven to 500F with Dutch oven
- 5:30 AM: Score and bake first 2 loaves (20 min covered, 20 min uncovered)
- 6:10 AM: Second pair
- 6:50 AM: Third pair
- 7:30 AM: Cool, pack up
- 7:45 AM: Leave for Groundwork
- 8:00 AM: Deliver

## Capacity Issues
- Only one Dutch oven -- can do 2 at a time max
- Need ~90 minutes of oven time for all 6
- Mixing 2700g of dough by hand is a workout
- Banneton situation: I have 3. Need 3 more or improvise with bowls + towels

## Test Plan
- Week of Jan 19: Test bake 6 loaves, follow timeline above
- Week of Jan 26: Second test bake, adjust timing
- Keep best 2, give rest to neighbors/church

notes.title: Delivery Schedule Draft
notes.content:
# Delivery Schedule Draft — Groundwork Coffee

## Weekly Schedule
| Day | Time | Task |
|---|---|---|
| Thursday PM | 8:00 PM | Feed starter |
| Friday PM | 10:00-11:00 PM | Mix dough, autolyse |
| Friday late / Sat early | 11 PM - 2 AM | Bulk ferment, shape |
| Saturday AM | 5:00-7:30 AM | Bake all 6 loaves |
| Saturday AM | 7:45 AM | Leave apartment |
| Saturday AM | 8:00 AM | Deliver to Groundwork |

## On-Call Conflict Plan
If on call during a bake week:
- Option A: Bake Wednesday night instead (bread slightly less fresh but still good)
- Option B: Scale down to 4 loaves if I lose time to incidents
- Option C: Text Jamie by Thursday if I cant deliver that week

Realistically I think I can handle this 3 out of 4 weeks. The 4th week (on-call) Ill play by ear.

## First Trial Deliveries
- Feb 7: First delivery
- Feb 14: Second
- Feb 21: Third
- Feb 28: Fourth
- Mar 7: Fifth and final trial delivery

## Payment
- Jamie sends Venmo after each delivery
- @jamie-linden-gw
- $90/week

## Notes
- Keep a delivery log (date, loaf count, any issues)
- Groundwork is 10 min from the apartment, no highway needed
- Parking on N High can be tricky at 8 AM on Saturday but should be fine that early
- Bring bread in paper bags, not plastic

notes.title: First Month Summary
notes.content:
# First Month Revenue/Cost Summary

Trial period: Feb 7 - Mar 7 (5 Saturdays)

## Revenue
| Date | Loaves | Amount |
|---|---|---|
| Feb 7 | 6 | $90 |
| Feb 14 | 6 | $90 |
| Feb 21 | 6 | $90 |
| Feb 28 | 6 | $90 |
| Mar 7 | 6 (pending) | $90 |
| **Total** | **30** | **$450** |

## Costs
| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| Flour (bread + whole wheat) | $38.50 |
| Parchment paper | $4.99 |
| Gas (5 trips x ~$1.60) | $8.00 |
| Extra bannetons (bought 3) | $27.00 |
| **Total** | **$78.49** |

## Net Profit: $371.51

## Time Investment
- ~5 hours per bake session (including baking time)
- ~30 min per delivery
- Total: ~27.5 hours over 5 weeks
- Hourly rate: ~$13.50/hour

## Pros
- Consistent extra income ($300+/month)
- Customers love it (Jamie says they sell out every week)
- I was baking anyway -- might as well get paid
- Goes straight to house fund

## Cons
- Friday nights are gone. Every week.
- Sleep is wrecked on bake nights
- If on-call week lands on a delivery week its brutal
- $13.50/hour is less than my day job. Is this worth my time?
- Trevor still doesnt know. How long can I keep this up?

## Decision
Need to think about this. The money is good but the schedule is unsustainable long-term, especially if I get the Ridgeline job (more demanding on-call? or less?).

Talk to Jamie about options -- maybe biweekly? Or find a backup baker for on-call weeks?
